title: Zero-waste kitchen swaps
subtitle: Reusable alternatives to the eight highest-turnover kitchen consumables
excerpt: Eight kitchen consumables account for the majority of household single-use plastic in the kitchen: cling film, kitchen roll, zip-lock bags, sponges, disposable cups, tea bags (some contain plastic mesh), baking parchment, and loose plastic bags at the checkout. Each has an established reusable alternative.
sourceNotes: WRAP kitchen plastics usage data. Zero Waste Week UK campaign data. Ethical Consumer kitchen product comparisons.

content.text: The priority list below is ordered by volume displaced per swap. The first three swaps (cling film, kitchen roll, zip-lock bags) account for most single-use plastic weight in a typical UK kitchen. Each swap has a payback period under two years compared to the consumable it replaces.

Beeswax wraps (cotton fabric coated with beeswax, jojoba oil, and tree resin) cover bowls and wrap sandwiches. Vegan alternative: plant-based wax wrap. Cost: for a pack of three. Lasts 12-18 months with normal use. Wash in cold water only, hot water melts the wax. Not suitable for raw meat.

Cut-up old cotton T-shirts or purpose-made cotton dishcloths replace most kitchen roll uses. A pack of 10 dishcloths costs and lasts years. For tasks where kitchen roll is better (absorbing cooking grease, handling raw meat), bamboo paper towels are an intermediate step, compostable and more absorbent than cotton.

Silicone reusable bags are functionally identical to zip-lock bags, freezer-safe, dishwasher-safe, and airtight. A set of four costs and lasts 3-5 years. Alternatively: glass or stainless steel containers with clip lids for the same use case with better durability.

Standard synthetic sponges shed microplastics with each wash and last 2-4 weeks. Alternatives: loofah (natural gourd, compostable at end of life), cellulose dishcloths (compostable), wooden dish brushes with replaceable heads. Cost is comparable; lifespan is longer for brushes.

Some branded tea bags contain polypropylene in the mesh and cannot be composted. Major brands have been moving to plant-based mesh, check the packaging or the brand's own guidance. Loose-leaf tea in a reusable infuser eliminates the packaging entirely and is cheaper per cup than most branded bags.

Silicone baking mats replace disposable parchment paper for the majority of baking and roasting. A standard mat costs and lasts hundreds of uses. Not suitable for all applications, very wet batters or extremely sticky items may still need disposable parchment.
